coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots combined with phytosphingosine inhibit acid-induced demineralization of hydroxyapatite in vitro
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Cuicui
Brand, Henk S.
Nazmi, Kamran
Werner, Arie
van Splunter, Annina
Bikker, Floris J.
coredata.dc:description:
Objectives To study the effects of carbon dots (CDs), in combination with phytosphingosine (PHS), against acid-induced demineralization of hydroxyapatite in vitro.
Methods CDs were generated from citric acid and urea by microwave heating. Transmission electron microscope (TEM), FT-IR, and fluorescence intensity were used to characterize the CDs. A hydroxyapatite (HAp) model was used to investigate the protective effects of CDs, PHS, and their combinations with and without a salivary pellicle against acid-induced demineralization in vitro. Ca2+ release as a parameter to evaluate the inhibition of demineralization was measured by capillary electrophoresis. The interactions between CDs, PHS, and HAp discs were investigated using a fluorescence detector.
Results Uniform-sized CDs were synthesized, showing typical optical characteristics. CDs exhibited no inhibition of acid-induced demineralization in vitro, in contrast to PHS. Notably, a pre-coating of CDs increased the protective effects of PHS against acid-induced demineralization, which was not disturbed by the presence of a salivary pellicle and Tween 20. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) confirmed the binding and layers formed of both CDs and PHS to the HAp surfaces. Based on fluorescence spectra CDs binding to HAp seemed to be dependent on Ca2+ and PO4 3- interactions.
Conclusions CDs combined with PHS showed protective effects against acid-induced demineralization of HAp discs in vitro.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Phytosphingosine
Acid-induced demineralization
Hydroxyapatite
Dental erosion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Protective effects of CDs and PHS against acid-induced demineralization were evaluated with and without a salivary pellicle
Protective effects of CDs and PHS against demineralization of HAp discs
Impact of CDs and PHS on demineralization of saliva-coated HAp discs
Morphology evaluation of HAp discs by SEM
Adsorption and binding between PHS and HAp discs in the absence and presence of CDs
Protective effects of CDs combined with PHS in Tween against demineralization of HAp discs
Interactions between CDs and HAp discs
Protective effects of CDs against demineralization of HAp discs
SEM Characterization of coating Layers on HAp discs
Evaluation of binding among CDs, PHS, and HAp discs

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Cuicui Fu is supported by China Scholarship Council (grant number 201908410228). We give special thanks to Xiaoyun Qin for her help in characterization of carbon dots.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characterization of CDs. (A) TEM of the CDs formed (yellow arrow). (B) Histogram of the particle size distributions of CDs. (C) The FTIR spectrum of CDs. (D) XRD pattern of CDs. (E) The UV-Vis spectrum of CDs. (F) The survey XPS spectra of CDs. High resolution spectra of (G) C1s, (H) N1s, and (I) O1s.

Optical properties of CDs. (A) Emission photoluminescence spectra of CDs with dependent excitation wavelength. (B) Graph: the ratio of A1 - A9 to normalized PL spectra of A0. The red line: the concentrations of the diluted CDs denoted as A0 (1 mg/mL) to A9 were half of the former one.

Anti-erosive properties of CDs combined with PHS in bare and saliva-coated HAp discs. (A) The normalized amount of Ca2+ loss from HAp discs incubated with CDs, PHS, and CDs-PHS, respectively. (B) The normalized amount of Ca2+ loss from saliva pre-incubated HAp discs were incubated with CDs, PHS, and CDs-PHS, respectively. Each bar denotes the average value of triplicate measurements; error bars show standard deviations (n = 3). * p < 0.05, * * p < 0.01.

Morphologies of HAp discs coated with different solutions. The surface morphologies of HAp discs coated with diH2O (A), CDs (B), PHS (C), and CDs-PHS (D), respectively, were examined by SEM. Scale bar: 1 µm.

Evaluation of binding among CDs, PHS, and HAp discs. (A) The amounts of adsorption and binding between PHS and HAp discs with or without CDs. (B) The normalized amount of Ca2+ loss of discs incubated with CDs-PHS in Tween and PHS in Tween, respectively. Each bar denotes the average value of triplicate measurements; error bars show standard deviations (n = 3). * p < 0.05; * * p < 0.01.
Evaluation of FL of interacted CDs. (A) The FL of CDs and CDs after incubation with HAp discs. (B) The FL of CDs with and without Na+ and Cl-, CDs combined with Ca2+, and CDs combined with PO4 3-.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots combined with phytosphingosine inhibit acid-induced demineralization of hydroxyapatite in vitro

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Oral Biochemistry, Academic Centre for Dentistry Amsterdam (ACTA), University of Amsterdam (UvA) and Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam (VU), Amsterdam 1081LA, the Netherlands

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Oral Biochemistry, Academic Centre for Dentistry Amsterdam (ACTA), University of Amsterdam (UvA) and Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam (VU)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Oral Biochemistry, Academic Centre for Dentistry Amsterdam (ACTA), University of Amsterdam (UvA) and Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam (VU), Amsterdam, 1081LA, the Netherlands

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Dental Materials Science, Academic Centre for Dentistry Amsterdam (ACTA), University of Amsterdam (UvA) and Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam (VU), Amsterdam 1081LA, the Netherlands

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Dental Materials Science, Academic Centre for Dentistry Amsterdam (ACTA), University of Amsterdam (UvA) and Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam (VU)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Dental Materials Science, Academic Centre for Dentistry Amsterdam (ACTA), University of Amsterdam (UvA) and Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam (VU), Amsterdam, 1081LA, the Netherlands

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To study the effects of carbon dots (CDs), in combination with phytosphingosine (PHS), against acid-induced demineralization of hydroxyapatite in vitro.

CDs were generated from citric acid and urea by microwave heating. Transmission electron microscope (TEM), FT-IR, and fluorescence intensity were used to characterize the CDs. A hydroxyapatite (HAp) model was used to investigate the protective effects of CDs, PHS, and their combinations with and without a salivary pellicle against acid-induced demineralization in vitro. Ca2+ release as a parameter to evaluate the inhibition of demineralization was measured by capillary electrophoresis. The interactions between CDs, PHS, and HAp discs were investigated using a fluorescence detector.

Uniform-sized CDs were synthesized, showing typical optical characteristics. CDs exhibited no inhibition of acid-induced demineralization in vitro, in contrast to PHS. Notably, a pre-coating of CDs increased the protective effects of PHS against acid-induced demineralization, which was not disturbed by the presence of a salivary pellicle and Tween 20. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) confirmed the binding and layers formed of both CDs and PHS to the HAp surfaces. Based on fluorescence spectra CDs binding to HAp seemed to be dependent on Ca2+ and PO4 3- interactions.
CDs combined with PHS showed protective effects against acid-induced demineralization of HAp discs in vitro.

ce:section.ce:para: Dental erosion is defined as the loss of dental hard tissue chemically etched away from the tooth surface (i.e., enamel or root surface) by acids without bacterial involvement (Imfeld, 1996; Lussi et al., 2009; Shellis, Featherstone, & Lussi, 2014). Epidemiological studies showed that the percentage of young people who have signs of dental erosion raises up to 30% (El Aidi et al., 2010; Nunn et al., 2003). The etiology of dental erosion is related to diverse biological, behavioral, and chemical factors, yet mainly attributed to acids originating from intrinsic sources such as gastroesophageal acid (Lussi, Jaeggi, & Zero, 2004; Scheutzel, 1996) and extrinsic risk factors such as dietary acidic beverages (Carvalho & Lussi, 2020; Søvik et al., 2015). Repeated and chronic exposure to acids in the oral cavity leads to the demineralization of hydroxyapatite (HAp), which is the main mineral component of the dental enamel. Subsequently, dental erosion will lead to an increased risk of tooth sensitivity, tooth discoloration, cavities, pain, tooth fractures, and functional impairments (Johansson et al., 2012; Schlueter, Jaeggi, & Lussi, 2012).

Consequently, clinicians and researchers have shown keen interest in developing preventive strategies for managing dental erosion. These comprehensive approaches encompass various measures, such as the exploration of re-mineralizing agents, for example, toothpaste, gels, and rinses that contain fluoride ions, calcium, phosphate, etc. (Amaechi & Higham, 2005; Buzalaf, Magalhães, & Wiegand, 2014). Although many of these approaches have shown promise in reducing the effects of acid-induced demineralization, none have proved to be a permanent solution. Research is therefore still needed to find alternative preventive strategies and therapies.

In this line, recent researches are directed to study the protective functions of phytosphingosine (PHS), a sphingoid-base, which occurs broadly in nature; including in saliva and at mucosal surfaces of the oral cavity (Fischer et al., 2013; Valentijn-Benz et al., 2015). PHS is characterized by its aliphatic hydrocarbon ramifications and the existence of positively charged functional groups and show affinity for phosphate ions in solution (Araujo et al., 2023; Sauro et al., 2016). Interestingly, it is found that PHS could form a protective coating against acid-induced damage on dental materials including: HAp discs and bovine enamel specimens (Amorim et al., 2021; Araujo et al., 2023; Yönel et al., 2016).

Besides, another promising carbon-based nanomaterial i.e., carbon dots (CDs), has attracted researchers’ attention. CDs are amorphous or nanocrystalline sp2 or sp3 carbon clusters with functional groups attached to their surfaces (Mansuriya & Altintas, 2021). Their synthesis, characterization, and applications have been widely investigated since first discovered in 2004 by Xu and co-workers (Wang et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2004; B. Yang, Jelinek, & Kang, 2020; Zuo et al., 2016). So far, CDs have been generated from various precursors, such as citric acid, collagen waste, and plants through hydrothermal or microwave-assisted methods (Fu et al., 2023; Qin et al., 2022; Schneider et al., 2017). Due to their unique characteristics, such as photoluminescence (PL), biocompatibility, physiochemical stability, and ease of derivatization, CDs have been investigated for various applications in biomedical areas and showed great potential in e.g. biosensing and bioimaging, drug delivery, and tissue engineering in general health as well as oral health (Fu, Brand, & Bikker, 2023; Ji et al., 2020; Liu, Li, & Yang, 2020). They show anti-microbial effects such as direct or indirect bactericidal potential toward Porphyromonas gingivalis, Streptococcus mutans, and Enterococcus faecalis (Liang et al., 2020; M. Liu et al., 2022b; Tang et al., 2022), oral cancer therapeutic effects in vitro mediated with photodynamic treatment (Kong et al., 2022; Q. Li et al., 2020; Nasrin, Hassan, & Gomes, 2020), and potential to promote cell differentiation and oral tissue regeneration in vitro and in vivo (L. L. Liu et al., 2022a; Lu et al., 2022). Interestingly, it was recently found that CDs can bind to HAp, and it was even proposed that they could potentially play a role in caries management by their antimicrobial activity (Yin, Mei, & Chu, 2023). This tempted us to investigate if their oral applications could be extended further to the protection against acid-induced demineralization in vitro, combined with PHS.

CDs were prepared by a microwave-assisted method according to a previous report (Simões, Leitão, & da Silva, 2016). 2 g citric acid (Sigma, St. Louis, MO, USA) and 2 g urea (Sigma) were dissolved in 20 mL diH2O and subsequently transferred to a Teflon beaker. The resulting solution was then subjected to microwave irradiation with a potency of 700 W for 5 min, by using a microwave oven (Clatronic, Kempen, Germany). After 10 min cooling at RT, 20 mL of diH2O was added to re-suspend the resulting precipitate. The supernatant was collected and filtrated with a 0.45 µm syringe filter (Sartorius, Göttingen, Germany). To eliminate suspended impurities, the filtrate was dialyzed against PBS in a 2000 MWCO dialysis bag (Thermo Scientific, Rockford, USA), and the PBS was refreshed every 12 h in 48 h. The liquid was dried overnight (ON) with a freeze-drier, CHRIST (Salm en Kipp, Utrecht, Netherlands). The weight of the obtained solids was measured with a precision weighing balance (Sartorius). Finally, the CDs were dispersed in diH2O with a concentration of 1.0 mg/mL for further use.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to verify the morphology of CDs. The CDs sample for TEM characterization was prepared by placing a drop of CDs solution on a carbon-coated copper grid and dried at RT for 30 min. The TEM images were recorded on Tecnai T12 electron microscopy (Thermo Fisher, Massachusetts, USA) with an accelerating voltage of 120 kV. The size distribution of CDs was calculated as described (Akbari, Tavandashti, & Zandrahimi, 2011). FT-IR spectra were performed on a Nicolet 5700 IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher) in the range of 400 − 4000 cm−1. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained on a Dmax-2500 X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Tokyo, Japan) with Cu Ka radiation (λ = 1.54 Å) at 50 kV and 200 mA at a scanning rate of 5° min−1. Evolution 201 Spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher) was used to obtain UV–Vis spectra. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was recorded on an Escalab MK II X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (VG Scientific Ltd., UK) using Mg2+ as the exciting source. Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded on an RF-6000 luminescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan) at RT in an aqueous solution whereas the excitation and emission wavelengths were measured from 300 – 540 nm and 300 – 800 nm, respectively. The fluorescence emission spectra of different concentrations of CDs were measured as well. The concentrations of the diluted CDs denoted as A0 – A9 were half of the concentration of the former one as shown in
Fig. 2B (red line). The emission wavelengths were obtained with the excitation wavelengths 420 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Protective effects of CDs and PHS against acid-induced demineralization were evaluated with and without a salivary pellicle
ce:section.ce:para: The susceptibility of HAp to an acid challenge was determined by measuring Ca2+ loss from HAp discs (Swerea, Stockholm, Sweden) after immersion in citric acid using a capillary electrophoresis (CE) system (CAPEL-205 Lumex Instruments Canada, Mission, BC, Canada) essentially as described elsewhere (Faruque et al., 2023). HAp discs were cleaned and rinsed with diH2O and dried at RT. They were placed in the wells of 12-well cell culture plates (Greiner Bio-One), to which 1 mL of the test solution or the control was added and incubated for 20 min on a shaker (150 rpm, 37 °C. BINDER, Tuttlingen, Germany). Discs were rinsed with diH2O (3 times) and then incubated in 1 mL of 0.1 M citric acid, pH = 3.7 on the shaker. The acid liquid was collected and discs were rinsed 3 times with 2 mL diH2O.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Protective effects of CDs and PHS against demineralization of HAp discs
ce:section.ce:para: The protection efficacy of CDs, PHS and their combimatiom towards the demineralization induced by citric acid of HAp discs was evaluated. At first, HAp discs were randomly divided into 4 groups: incubated with the presence of diH2O (Blank), CDs, PHS, and CDs-PHS (pre-treated with CDs 20 min, after washing subsequently incubated with PHS), respectively. PHS was obtained from (Doosan Corporation, France) and 100 μg/mL solutions were prepared as described before (Valentijn-Benz et al., 2015), and CDs were dissolved in diH2O at concentration of 1 mg/mL. After incubating with test solutions, HAp discs were washed 3 times with diH2O, and incubated with 1 mL citric acid on a shaker. The acid liquid was collected for measuring Ca2+ loss using CE as described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of CDs and PHS on demineralization of saliva-coated HAp discs
ce:section.ce:para: In vitro saliva-coated HAp discs were achieved as follows: unstimulated whole saliva (UWS) was collected without conscious stimulation, and informed consent was obtained from the donor (female, 32 years old). The collection of saliva was approved by the local Ethical Board of ACTA (protocol numbers: 202094 and 2022–52738). Saliva was homogenized by vortexing and then centrifuged at 20,000 g for 10 min to remove cells and debris. The clear supernatant was used directly in the experiments. HAp discs (HiMed, New York, USA) were incubated with 1 mL of UWS for 1 h in 12 well plates (Greiner Bio One) at RT. After incubation, discs were rinsed 3 times with dH2O to remove unbound protein. Saliva-treated discs were incubated with diH2O, CDs, PHS, and CDs-PHS as described above. The susceptibility to an acid challenge was then determined as described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology evaluation of HAp discs by SEM
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies of HAp discs incubated with diH2O, CDs, PHS, and CDs-PHS were evaluated. The incubated discs were sputter-coated with gold and examined using SEM (EVO LS15, Carl Zeiss, Oberkochen, Germany) at 25 K and 100 K maginifications and at an accelerating voltage of 20 kV. The regions of specimens were selected randomly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption and binding between PHS and HAp discs in the absence and presence of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: HAp discs (HiMed) were placed in the wells of 12-well cell culture plates (Greiner Bio-One), to which 1 mL of the CDs-PHS solutions (pre-treated with CDs 20 min, after washing subsequently incubated with PHS) or PHS only solutions were added and incubated on the shaker. After 20 mins incubation, the left solution in plates was collected. To 100 μL of this solution, 25 μL ortho-phthalaldehyde reagent (OPA, Sigma-Aldrich) was added to enable fluorimetric quantification of PHS. Fluorescence was measured in a Fluostar Galaxy microplate fluorimeter (BMFG LABTECH, Offenburg, Germany) at excitation and emission wavelengths of 380 and 450 nm, respectively, essentially as described earlier (Valantij-Benz 2015). Absolute quantities were determined by reference to a standard curve of PHS.

Discs were rinsed with 4 mL buffer (3 times) and demineralized water (2 times). PHS was extracted from the discs by incubation in 1.5 mL ethanol for 10 min. After evaporation, the residue was dissolved in 250 μL of ethanol. The quantification of PHS was measured as above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Protective effects of CDs combined with PHS in Tween against demineralization of HAp discs
ce:section.ce:para: Stock solutions of PHS were prepared in ethanol at a concentration of 1 mg/mL. The stock solution was further diluted in 0.1% Tween 20 (pH 6.8) to the indicated concentrations. diH2O, CDs-PHS in Tween, and PHS in Tween were added to the HAp discs in the wells of a 12-well cells culture plate (Bio-One), respectively. After incubating discs, acid solutions were collected and Ca2+ loss was measured as described above using CE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interactions between CDs and HAp discs
ce:section.ce:para: To study the interactions between CDs and HAp discs, HAp discs (HiMed) were placed in the wells of 12-well cell culture plates (Greiner Bio-One), to which 1 mL of the CDs solutions were added and incubated on the shaker. After 20 mins incubation, the CDs and left solution in plates were collected to measure fluorescence emission spectra. To verify the interactions between Ca2+ or PO4 3-, 10 mM CaCl2, 10 mM Na3PO4, and 10 mM NaCl were added to CDs respectively to measure fluorescence emission spectra using RF-6000 luminescence spectrometer (Shimadzu).

Data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation and each experiment was repeated three times. The data were analyzed by one-way ANOVA by GraphPad Prism 8.0 (GraphPad Software Inc., La Jolla, CA, USA). A p-value < 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

The morphology and particle size of the prepared CDs were characterized by TEM, which revealed that the CDs (yellow arrows) can be well-dispersed with a narrow size distribution (Fig. 1A). CDs were well dispersed with an average statistical diameter of 10.58 ± 1.51 nm (Fig. 1B).

The FT-IR spectra shown in Fig. 1C exhibited a typical broad absorption around 3000–3500 cm−1 due to stretching vibrations of–OH or–NH, which originated from the terminal hydroxyl or amino groups in the CDs. The band at 1699 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibration of CO groups. The absorption peak at 1576 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibration of CC groups in the aromatic structure. The peak around 1355 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibration of C-N groups in amines. The peak at 1183 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibration of C-O-C in the ester structure.

The XRD pattern of CDs (Fig. 1D) revealed a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 21.1° attributed to amorphous carbon phase. As shown in Fig. 1E, the UV–vis spectrum of the CDs aqueous solution exhibited broad absorption peaks around 335 nm and 395 nm can be assigned to n-π * transition of multi-conjugate functional groups with unsaturated graphite carbon. CDs were mainly composed of C, N, and O elements (Fig. 1F). The peak compositions of the three elements were further analyzed by high resolution XPS spectra. The deconvolution analysis of C1s spectrum shows three peaks at 284.2, 285, and 286.9 eV, belonging to C-C/CC, C-N/C-O, and CO groups, respectively (Fig. 1G). The N1s peaks corresponding to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N were observed at 397.8, 399.4, and 401.3 eV, respectively (Fig. 1H). The two peaks in O1s spectrum located at 531.7 eV and 533.2 eV can be assigned to CO and C-OH/C-O-C groups (Fig. 1I).

To explore typical characteristics of CD’s, the optical properties i.e., their PL emission spectra were recorded (Fig. 2A). Along with the changed excitation wavelength from 300 to 540 nm, the PL peak shifted from 510 (red) to 520 nm (blue). The optimum emissive center of the CDs sample was observed at 540 nm. Furthermore, to confirm the PL emission spectra of CDs at different concentrations, a series of CDs at various concentrations was prepared for observation (Fig. 2B, red line). The FL intensity increased with the decrease in concentration of CDs from A0 to A2 in the solvent and it was 5.8 times higher in A2 than A0, while FL intensities of A2 and A3 were comparable. The FL intensity gradually decreased with the decrease in concentration of CDs from A4 to A9 in the solvent (Fig. 2B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Protective effects of CDs against demineralization of HAp discs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the protective effects of CDs and PHS against demineralization, HAp discs were divided into 4 groups: blank, CDs, PHS, and CDs-PHS. As shown in Fig. 3A and Fig. 3B, the presence of CDs did not lead to the protection against acid induced demineralization on both bare and saliva coated HAp discs. The prersence of PHS lead to a decreased level of Ca2+ loss compared to the blank group on bare HAp discs to 64% (Fig. 3A). The combination of CDs and PHS resulted in a further decrease in Ca2+ loss compared to the blank group on bare HAp discs to 26% (Fig. 3A). CDs-PHS significantly inhibited bare HAp discs from demineralization, indicating that CDs pretreated the HAp discs can effectively enhance the function of PHS and protect HAp discs against acid erosion.

Furthermore, comparable results were observed when HAp discs were pre-coated with saliva (Fig. 3B). It showed that in the presence of CDs, PHS, and CDs-PHS, Ca2+ loss on saliva-coated HAp discs decreased to 83.3%, 63.7%, and 53.3%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SEM Characterization of coating Layers on HAp discs
ce:section.ce:para: The intractions of CDs and PHS with HAp discs were visualized using SEM. As shown in Fig. 4, the surface of HAp disc was smooth and uncovered in the blank group (Fig. 4A). Discs treated with CDs were homogeneously covered by nanoparticles and these dots formed a layer on the surface of HAp discs (Fig. 4B). In the PHS group, part of HAp discs was covered by larger white particles compared to CDs (Fig. 4C). Furthermore, the presence of relative smooth, medium-sized particels and layers (white arrows) was observed on the surface of HAp discs pretreated with CDs-PHS (Fig. 4D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of binding among CDs, PHS, and HAp discs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the interactions between PHS and HAp discs in the presence of CDs, the adsorbed amount of PHS and the binding characterisatics between PHS and HAp discs were analysed. Fig. 5A showed no significant differences in the weight of total adsorbed PHS in discs and the amount of bound PHS were observed in the CDs-PHS and PHS group.

To confirm the protection of PHS in Tween-20 combined with CDs against demineralization, Ca2+ losses were calculated accordingly. As shown in Fig. 5B, the protection by CDs-PHS was not abolished in the presence of 1% Tween-20, indicating that Tween did not affect CDs-PHS mediated protection.

To examine whether the observed protective effects came from the interactions between CDs and HAp discs, we tested FL difference of CDs before and after incubation with discs. Fig. 6A showed the FL intensity after incubation with HAp discs was 1.5 times lower than that before incubation with HAp discs, revealing CDs-HAp interactions. The FL intensity was increased when adding Ca2+ in CDs solution and FL intensity was decreased when adding PO4 3- in CDs solution, which indicates interactions between CDs and Ca2+ and PO4 3- (Fig. 6B). The presence of Na+ and Cl-, which were the counter ions of PO43- and Ca2+ respectively, did not affect the FL of CDs.

In this study, it was found that a pre-coating of CDs on bare HAp discs promoted the anti-erosive effects of PHS against demineralization induced by acid in vitro; Ca2+ loss in HAp discs treated with CDs-PHS was significantly decreased in the presence of CDs compared to PHS only on bare discs with and without Tween.

Based on a previous report, we found that CDs synthesized by microwave approach showed uniformed size, besides, the FT-IR spectra suggested that there were abundant amino, carboxyl, and other hydrophilic groups on its surface, contributing to good water solubility (Qin et al., 2022). The excitation-dependent PL behavior was extensively reported in fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials, and it may have resulted from the optical selection of differently sized CDs and surface defects of CDs. Microwave-assisted synthesis of CDs offers distinct advantages such as lower reaction time and instances of side reactions and more uniform physical, chemical, and optical properties in comparison to hydrothermal techniques. Nonetheless, this method does come with its drawbacks. Microwave reactors typically have limitations in terms of volume, which restricts their applicability in large-scale reactions (Dąbrowska et al., 2018; Priecel & Lopez-Sanchez, 2018).

In this study an in vitro erosion model was used based on densely compressed HAp discs as a substitute for tooth mineral as they have standardized morphology, crystallinity, and chemical composition (Ca10(PO4)6(OH)2) (Imthiaz et al., 2008; Okada & Matsumoto, 2015). This model has been used by others showing low experimental variability and dissolution rates (Ronay et al., 2019) and can be used as an enamel analog as underlined in various in vitro demineralization studies (Araujo et al., 2023; Blumhagen et al., 2014; Yönel et al., 2016).

Earlier it was found that PHS showed a protective function against acid-induced demineralization in vitro (Valentijn-Benz et al., 2015; Yönel et al., 2016). In the present study, we proposed novel treatment options to protect the enamel: using PHS solution and CDs separately as well as their combinations. Interestingly, previously Yang and coworkers found that CDs we involved in HAp nucleation and crysal growth small HAp nanoparticles were formed on surface carboxyl-functionalized CDs (H. Yang et al., 2018). Inspired by this work, we found that CDs combined with PHS, the Ca2+ loss was significantly decreased on the surface of bare HAp discs. This finding is consistent with the study of Ca2+ ions replaced positive ions on the CDs in situ to form CDs–Ca precipitates and resulting electrostatic force enabled phosphate ions to bond with CDs–Ca precipitates (H. Yang et al., 2018). Typically, the surfaces of CDs contain a large number of carboxylic acid moieties, making them suitable for subsequent functionalization with organic, polymeric, or inorganic species (H. Li et al., 2012; Roy et al., 2015). Moreover, the interactions between CDs and HAp were investigated by Khajuria and coworkers who found that CDs were bound to HAp by electrostatic interaction and H-bonding, and CDs–HAp nanoparticles significantly enhanced bone regeneration and mineral density compared to HAp nanoparticles (Khajuria et al., 2018). In the current study it was found that low levels of Tween 20 did not disturb the protective effects of PHS and CDs, indicating that the mechanism of binding between CDs and HAp discs seems to be dependent on the type of CDs, and therefore needs further detailed investigation.

By using SEM it was observed that layers were formed on the surface of HAp discs when treated with different solutions. A previous study showed that PHS exhibited the capability to create protective multilayers on HAp, functioning as effective diffusion barriers against H + ions (Valentijn-Benz et al., 2015). Interestingly, this layer also prevented the absorption to bacteria to HAp and glass surfaces under both static and dynamic conditions (Bikker et al., 2018; Cukkemane et al., 2015).

In situ, salivary proteins adhere to the HAp surface to form an acquired salivary pellicle, which, to a certain extend, prevents the surface from making direct contact with acids and protects it from erosive demineralization (Dawes et al., 2015). Moreover, acquired salivary pellicle contributes to neutralizing the acidity from the oral environment by modulating calcium and phosphate concentrations on the enamel surface (Chawhuaveang et al., 2021; Dawes et al., 2015; Hannig & Hannig, 2014). In addition, saliva contains calcium-binding proteins, such as mucins, histatins, and acid proline-rich proteins, which adjust the concentration of calcium on the tooth surface to a supersaturated level to prevent the further dissolution of HAp (Vukosavljevic et al., 2014). Altogether, calcium ions are playing a versatile role at the microenvironment and the dental surface. On the one hand, results in this study revealed that the Ca2+ losses on saliva-pre-coated HAp discs treated with PHS and CDs-PHS were decreased, indicating that PHS is compatible with the salivary pellicle, rendering potential in vivo applications more plausible. On the other hand however, the loss of CDs-PHS synergy in the presence of a salivary pellicle seems to be lost compared to bare HAp, which potentially can be explained by an altered Ca2+ availablility by pellicle proteins, yet needs to be investigated.

CDs generated from citric acid and urea showed anti-demineralization effects combined with PHS on the surface of HAp discs in vitro. CDs seem to promote PHS binding to HAp leading to decreasing Ca2+ loss on the surface of HAp discs. CDs show potential interaction between Ca2+ and PO4 3- which makes CDs promising and attractive candidates as ingredients in oral care products against dental erosion.

Cuicui Fu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Henk S. Brand: Conceptualization, Methodology. Kamran Nazmi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation. Arie Werner: Methodology, Investigation. Annina van Splunter: Methodology, Investigation. Floris J. Bikker: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Cuicui Fu is supported by China Scholarship Council (grant number 201908410228). We give special thanks to Xiaoyun Qin for her help in characterization of carbon dots.
